content: Diagnostic approach: Confirm true hyperkalemia (repeat level, assess for hemolysis). Obtain immediate ECG for any K+ >6.0 or symptomatic patient. Assess renal function (BUN, creatinine), acid-base status (bicarbonate, ABG if severe), glucose. Evaluate medication list thoroughly. For persistent unexplained hyperkalemia: measure plasma renin activity and aldosterone to diagnose hypoaldosteronism (low aldosterone with high renin suggests primary adrenal insufficiency; low aldosterone with low renin suggests hyporeninemic hypoaldosteronism/type 4 RTA). Calculate transtubular potassium gradient (TTKG) if uncertain about renal potassium handling: TTKG = (urine K × serum osmolality)/(serum K × urine osmolality); <7 suggests impaired renal excretion. Consider cortisol level if adrenal insufficiency suspected. Evaluate for rhabdomyolysis (CK), tumor lysis (LDH, uric acid, phosphate) if clinically indicated.
